a: Wi-Fi (802.11), Cellular (4G/5G), Bluetooth (short-range device pairing), NFC (very short-range, e.g., tap payments), and GPS (location).

q: WPA2 vs. WPA3 — what's the key improvement?
a: WPA3 is the current standard. It replaces WPA2's PSK 4-way handshake with SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals), protecting against offline password cracking and adding forward secrecy.

q: What is WPA personal (PSK) vs. WPA enterprise?
a: Personal/PSK uses one shared pre-shared key for everyone. Enterprise uses 802.1X with a RADIUS server so each user authenticates with their own credentials (much more secure and manageable).
Simultaneous Authentication of Equals — WPA3's secure handshake (a.k.a. Dragonfly) that resists offline dictionary attacks and provides forward secrecy, replacing WPA2's PSK handshake.

q: Why should WEP never be used?
a: Wired Equivalent Privacy uses weak RC4 encryption and a flawed IV scheme that's trivially cracked in minutes — it's completely insecure and obsolete.

q: What is EAP and name common variants?
a: Extensible Authentication Protocol — an authentication framework used with 802.1X. Variants include EAP-TLS (certificate-based, strongest), PEAP, EAP-TTLS, and EAP-FAST.

q: What is an evil twin attack?
a: An attacker sets up a rogue access point that impersonates a legitimate Wi-Fi network (same SSID) to trick users into connecting so traffic can be intercepted.

q: What is a rogue access point?
a: An unauthorized AP connected to a network (by an attacker or careless employee) that creates an insecure entry point bypassing network controls.
Sending forged deauth/disassociation frames to knock clients off a Wi-Fi network — used for denial of service or to force reconnection to an evil twin.
Bluejacking sends unsolicited messages to a Bluetooth device (annoying but harmless). Bluesnarfing steals data from a Bluetooth device (a real breach).

q: What is a site survey and a heat map?
a: A site survey is a physical walkthrough using tools to map existing wireless signals, coverage, and interference. A heat map visually shows signal strength across the area to guide AP placement.

q: Define the mobile deployment models BYOD, COPE, CYOD, and COBO.
a: BYOD = employees use personal devices. COPE = corporate-owned, personally enabled (some personal use allowed). CYOD = choose your own device from an approved list. COBO = corporate-owned, business only.
Mobile Device Management / Unified Endpoint Management — software to centrally configure, secure, monitor, and control mobile (and other) devices, including pushing apps and remote wiping.
Installing apps from outside the official app store, bypassing its vetting — increasing the risk of installing malicious software.
Removing manufacturer restrictions to gain root/admin access on a mobile device. It bypasses the built-in security model, creating significant security risk.

q: What is containerization on mobile devices?
a: Separating corporate data/apps from personal data into an isolated, managed 'container' on the device so the company can secure/wipe work data without touching personal data.
An MDM capability to remotely erase data from a lost or stolen device (or just the corporate container) to prevent data exposure.
Using GPS/location to define a virtual boundary and trigger policies (e.g., restrict access or disable features) based on whether a device is inside or outside an area.

q: Why is public/open Wi-Fi a security risk for mobile users?
a: Traffic can be intercepted via on-path/evil-twin attacks. Users should use a VPN and HTTPS, and avoid sensitive transactions on untrusted networks.
